answer:
Iqbal's khudi verse: Bang-e-Dara Part 3, Chapter 12 ('Insaan'), paragraph p-cb3c43 — 'Whatever exists in the world is accustomed to submission; / Every power of the Human Being is aflame with striving.' The poem sets up a direct contrast: the Narcissus 'deprived of action is a compelled spectator,' the Pine's 'very nature is devoid of longing,' but the human being alone burns with striving and, crucially (p-587d8c), 'If it so chooses, it can change the very form of the garden; / This being is wise, is perceptive, is potent.' The self is not assigned; it is the very act of striving.

Manu's varna verse: Manusmrti Chapter 1, verse 98, paragraph p-307a84 — 'The very birth of a Brahmana is an eternal incarnation of Dharma; for he is born for the sake of Dharma and is destined to become one with Brahma.' The preceding verse (Mn_1.31, p-13a2db) establishes the cosmogonic basis: the four varnas emanated from Brahma's mouth, arms, thighs, and feet. Verse 98 draws the sharpest conclusion: birth itself is not a precondition of dharma but its 'eternal incarnation.' The Brahmin is born supreme (Mn_1.99, p-6ba49e), and by 'pre-eminence of birth' is entitled to the whole world (Mn_1.100, p-cc748e). Social location is given, not built.

Which is harder to hold honestly? Manu's. Iqbal's khudi is demanding — the self must ceaselessly strive or it fails to become — but its claim is falsifiable in the right direction: show the person who strove and you confirm it, show the person who did not and you also confirm it. The doctrine is coherent with experience. Manu's birth-claim is harder because it assigns permanent, cosmic rank from the moment of birth and then must explain away every Brahmin who fails and every Shudra who excels. Manu himself quietly concedes the fracture (Mn_10.65: 'A Shudra attains the state of a Brahmana, and a Brahmana attains the state of a Shudra'), but this admission guts the foundational claim. To hold that 'the very birth of a Brahmana is an eternal incarnation of Dharma' while also acknowledging downward and upward mobility requires either a theology that renders counter-evidence invisible or an equivocation on what 'birth' and 'Dharma' mean. Khudi's self-construction is hard to live up to; varna-by-birth is hard to believe.

tool_calls.name: search_corpus
tool_calls.result_summary: 17 results but all in Ghalib/Zauq, not Iqbal — 'bekhudi' used in Sufi dissolution sense, not Iqbal's khudi

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Found key varna passages: Mn_1.31 (four varnas from body parts), Mn_1.87, Mn_1.98 (birth = eternal incarnation of Dharma), Mn_1.99-100 (born supreme by birth)

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Found 'Insaan' (The Human Being) — key passage p-cb3c43: 'Every power of the Human Being is aflame with striving'; p-587d8c: 'If it so chooses, it can change the very form of the garden'

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Khitab ba Jawanan — address to Muslim youth, collective identity theme, not specifically khudi self-construction

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Sarguzasht-e-Adam — Adam as restless self-constructor; 'This earth into a rival of Paradise I wrought' supports khudi but less direct

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Koshish-e-Natamam (Perpetual Striving) — 'Through striving without end, all living things remain'; supports theme

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Chapter on mixed varnas; Mn_10.59: 'one of bad origin can in no way conceal his own nature'; Mn_10.65 admits mobility but this weakens the birth claim

tool_calls.name: search_corpus
tool_calls.result_summary: Confirmed p-cb3c43 in Insaan ch.12 Bang-e-Dara Part 3: 'Every power of the Human Being is aflame with striving'
